Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours each day, but then I'd get distracted or overwhelmed and end up cramming everything into long sessions on weekends. I'm a visual learner, so I spent a lot of time making diagrams and color-coded notes, but the actual scheduling part never really clicked for me. With eight online courses, I was constantly juggling and never felt like I had a real system.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how anxious I was feeling about it. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then suddenly I'd realize deadlines were approaching and I'd panic-study for hours. I tried setting regular times, but life kept getting in the way—work stuff, personal things, just general exhaustion. The anxiety made it hard to stick to any kind of consistent schedule.
Q3: Usually it was either panic about a deadline or getting a notification that something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which honestly added to my stress more than it helped. The gamified tasks they introduced actually got me logging in a bit more because there was something slightly satisfying about completing those little challenges, even if I didn't always follow through on the bigger assignments.
Q4: There was a brief period where I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee. It lasted maybe two weeks before things got hectic again. The gamified elements helped make it feel less like a chore, but once my anxiety spiked about other things, even that routine fell apart. I wish I could say it became automatic, but it never really stuck.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline because I'm such a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations. I also found the discussion boards surprisingly helpful when other students explained concepts in different ways. Those peer interactions gave me perspectives I wouldn't have gotten just from the lectures alone.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They felt overwhelming and I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. Some of the optional quizzes I also skipped because they didn't count toward my grade and I was already stretched thin. When you're managing eight courses, you start triaging what actually matters for your final grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it connected to something I'd be tested on. The gamified tasks were good because they gave instant feedback and made me feel like I accomplished something small. If something felt abstract or disconnected from the actual assessments, I'd lose interest quickly because my mental energy was already so limited.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones came with this weight of anxiety and obligation, which honestly sometimes made me avoid them too. But at least with required activities, the fear of failing eventually pushed me to complete them. Optional stuff just got lost in the shuffle because there was no immediate consequence for skipping it.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lecture slides and videos to my laptop and studied them offline constantly. I'd print out diagrams and annotate them by hand because that's how information sticks for me. I also used YouTube videos and external websites to understand concepts the course materials didn't explain well enough. A lot of my real studying happened completely outside the LMS.
Q10: I'd spend hours going through my printed notes and hand-drawn diagrams at coffee shops or on my couch, none of which showed up anywhere. Sometimes I'd watch a video once in the LMS, then replay it from my downloads five more times while taking notes. I also did study sessions with a friend where we'd quiz each other, but that was all invisible to the system.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or video, open it once in the LMS so it counted as accessed, then never go back to the platform for that material. My offline folder was basically my real classroom. It was easier to focus without the distractions of notifications and the stress of seeing all my incomplete tasks staring back at me.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety was through the roof, I wasn't sleeping well, and everything felt impossible. There were weeks where I'd open the LMS, see all the things I was behind on, and just close it immediately because I couldn't handle the overwhelm. The more I fell behind, the harder it was to re-engage because the guilt just compounded.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed about other life stuff, the LMS became this thing I dreaded opening. Confusion about assignment requirements made me procrastinate even more because I didn't know where to start. Having eight courses meant I was constantly choosing which one to neglect, and sometimes this course lost that battle.
Q14: More times than I want to admit. I'd scroll through discussion posts or watch videos while my mind was completely elsewhere, just going through the motions. It was like my body was present but my brain had checked out. Those sessions felt productive in the moment but I'd retain almost nothing afterward.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like this huge mountain I couldn't climb. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel the same or worse. It's a terrible cycle and I know it hurt my completion rate badly.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit anything. I'd ignore tasks until they became urgent, then scramble at the last minute. The LMS would show these spikes of activity right before due dates, which probably looked erratic. Without deadlines, I honestly might not have submitted anything at all.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it motivated me to try harder on the next thing. But sometimes feedback came too late to feel relevant, or I was already so behind that it just added to my shame. Positive feedback gave me a brief boost, but it didn't last long before the anxiety crept back in.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having everything laid out in modules was good for my visual brain, but seeing all eight courses' worth of modules made me want to hide. The gamified elements helped break things into smaller pieces, which worked better for my scattered attention span.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier because they felt achievable and gave quick rewards. What made it harder was when instructions were vague or when I couldn't figure out how different pieces connected. Also, having so many resources available paradoxically made it harder because I didn't know what to prioritize.
Q20: That's basically me sometimes. I'd log in, watch videos, read discussions, click around trying to understand things, but then freeze when it came to actually submitting work. The anxiety around assignments being graded made submission feel terrifying, even when I'd done the learning. Activity doesn't equal output when fear is involved.
Q21: Yeah, that resonates. I managed a Merit grade despite my low assignment completion because I actually learned the material—just not always through the LMS. My exam scores were decent because I studied hard in my own way, offline, with my visual notes and diagrams. The system doesn't capture that kind of effort.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked, but they can't show understanding or effort. They miss all the offline studying, the mental processing, the anxiety that keeps you from engaging even when you want to. They're like measuring someone's fitness by counting how many times they walked into a gym.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and putting in effort to understand it, even when it's hard. It's not just about clicking through screens or hitting deadlines. For me, real engagement happens when I'm making connections, creating visual representations, and feeling like the knowledge is actually sinking in.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much I struggled with anxiety while trying to engage, or how much learning happened away from the screen. It doesn't capture the hours I spent with printed materials, the peer conversations, or the genuine interest I had despite my completion numbers being low. My engagement was real—it just didn't fit the system's definition.
